chunk_summary: Preterm infants with chronic lung disease (CLD/BPD) require targeted nutrition to address high metabolic bone disease risk, focusing on calcium, phosphorus, and vitamin D supplementation alongside monitoring actionable markers like ALP and serum phosphate.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of calcium, phosphorus, and vitamin D in managing metabolic bone disease in preterm CLD/BPD.
Learn numeric intake targets for calcium (120–220 mg/kg/day), phosphorus (70–120 mg/kg/day), and vitamin D (400 IU/day).
Identify actionable markers such as ALP (>800 IU/L) and serum phosphate (<4.5 mg/dL) for monitoring bone health in high-risk infants.
Explore strategies for optimizing mineral density in fluid-restricted preterm infants.
key_facts: Preterm CLD/BPD increases metabolic bone disease risk due to interrupted mineral accretion and therapies like loop diuretics and steroids.
Calcium intake targets: 120–220 mg/kg/day; phosphorus intake targets: 70–120 mg/kg/day.
Vitamin D supplementation: 400 IU/day, with dose adjustments based on levels and risk factors.
Actionable markers include ALP (>800 IU/L) and serum phosphate (<4.5 mg/dL).
Fluid-restricted infants require high-density fortifiers or formulas to optimize mineral intake per mL.
Bone health is integral to respiratory care, as fractures and chest wall deformities can worsen outcomes.
reference_quote: Metabolic bone disease of prematurity: diagnosis and management review — Faienza et al. (https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6474071/).
document_key_topics: Electrolyte and micronutrient management in preterm infants
Vitamin D, calcium, and phosphate targets for bone health
